upbraid: To charge with something wrong or disgraceful; to reproach; to cast in the teeth; followed by with or for, before the thing imputed; as, to upbraid a man for his folly or his intemperance. Yet do not upbraid us with our distress. He upbraided them with their unbelief. Wark 16:14.
To reproach; to chide. God who giveth to all men liberally, and upbraideth not. James 1:5.
To reprove with severity. Then he began to upbraid the cities wherein most of his mighty works were done - Matthew 17:20.
To bring reproach on. How much doth thy kindness upbraid my wickedness!
upbraided: Charged with something wrong or disgraceful; reproached; reproved.
upbraiding: Accusing; casting in the teeth; reproaching; reproving.
A charging with something wrong or disgraceful; the act of reproaching or reproving. I have too long borne your blunt upbraidings.
The reproaches or accusations of conscience.
upbray: for upbraid, to shame, is not in use.
upcast: Cast up; a term in bowling.
Thrown upwards; as with upcast eyes.
In bowling, a cast; a throw.
upheld: and pp. of uphold. Sustained; supported.
uphill: Difficult, like the act of ascending a hill; as uphill labor.
uphold: To lift on high; to elevate.
To support; to sustain; to keep from falling or slipping. Honor shall uphold the humble in spirit. Proverbs 29:23.
upholder: One that upholds; a supporter; a defender; a sustainer.
An undertaker; one who provides for funerals.
upholsterer: One who furnishes houses with beds, curtains and the like.
upland: High land; ground elevated above the meadows and intervals which lie on the banks of rivers, near the sea, or between hills; land which is generally dry. It is opposed to meadow, march, swamp, intervalUplands are particularly valuable as affording pasture for sheep.
Higher in situation; being on upland; as upland inhabitants.
Pertaining to uplands; as upland pasturage.
uplandish: Pertaining to uplands; dwelling on high lands or mountains.
uplift: To raise aloft; to raise; to elevate; as, to uplift the arm. It is chiefly used in the participle; as uplifted eyes; uplifted arms.
upon: Resting or being on the top or surface; as being upon a hill, or upon a rock; upon a field; upon a table; upon a river; upon the altar; upon the roof. He has his coat upon his back; his hat is upon his head.
In a state of resting or dependence; as upon this condition; he will contract with you upon these terms. Upon our repentance we hope to be forgiven.
Denoting resting, as a burden. Impose upon yourself this task.
In the direction or part of; as upon the right hand.
Relating to. They are now engaged upon the affairs of the bank.
upper: Higher in place; as the upper lip; the upper side of a thing. An upper story is a higher one; the upper story is the highest. So the upper deck of a ship.

Superior in rank or dignity; as the upper house of a legislature. Upper hand, advantage; superiority. Upper-works, in a ship, the parts above water when the ship is properly balanced for a voyage; or that part which is above the main wale.
uppermost: Highest in place; as the uppermost seats.z
Highest in power or authority. Whatever faction happens to be uppermost - .
upraise: s as z. To raise; to lift up.
upright: Erect; perpendicular to the plane of the horizon; as an upright tree; an upright post. Among mechanics.
Erected; pricked up; shooting directly from the body. All have their ears upright - . With chatt’ring teeth and bristling hair upright.
Honest; just; adhering to rectitude in all social intercourse; not deviating from correct moral principles; as an upright man. Job 1:8.
Conformable to moral rectitude. Conscience rewards upright conduct with pleasure.
uprightly: In a direction perpendicular to the plane of the horizon; in an erect position.
Honestly; with strict observance of rectitude; as, to live uprightly. He that walketh uprightly, walketh surely. Proverbs 10:9.
uprightness: Honesty; integrity in principle or practice; conformity to rectitude and justice in social dealings. The truly upright man is inflexible in his uprightness.
uprise: Ss as z. pret uprose; pp. uprisen.
To rise from bed or from a seat. Uprose the virgin with the morning light.
To ascend above the horizon. Uprose the sun.
To ascend, as a hill. Obs.
A rising; appearance above the horizon. Obs.
uprising: The act of rising. Thou knowest my down-sitting and mine uprising. PsaIm 139:2.
uproar: Great tumult; violent disturbance and noise; bustle and clamor. The Jews who believed not - set all the city in an uproar. Acis 17:5. Horror thus prevail’d, and wild uproar. / .
uproot: To root up; to tear up by the roots; as, to uproot the hills or trees.
uprouse: uprouz. To rouse from sleep; to awake.
upset: To overturn; to overthrow; to overset; as a carriage.
upshot: Final issue; conclusion; end; as the upshot of the matter. Here is the upshot and result of all. Upside down, the upper part undermost. As a phrase, this denotes in confusion; in complete disorder.
upstart: To start or spring up suddenly.
One that suddenly rises from low life to wealth, power or honor.
upturn: To turn up; to throw up; as, to upturn the ground in plowing.
upward: Directed to a higher place; as with upward eye; with upward speed.
Toward a higher place; opposed to downward. Upward I lift my eye.
Toward heaven and God. Looking inward, we are struck dumb; looking upward, we speak and prevail.
With respect to the higher part. Upward man, downward fish.
upwards: Toward a higher place; opposed to downward. Upward I lift my eye.
Toward heaven and God. Looking inward, we are struck dumb; looking upward, we speak and prevail.
With respect to the higher part. Upward man, downward fish.
More thanUpwards of ten years have elapsed; upwards of a hundred men were present.
Toward the source. Trace the stream upwards. And trace the muses upwards to their spring.
upwhirl: upwhurl’. To rise upwards in a whirl; to whirl upwards.
To raise upwards in a whirling direction.
uran-glimmer: An ore of uranium; uran-mica; chalcolite.
uranite: An ore or phosphate of uranium, called also uran-glimmer, and uran-mica. It is of a lemon yellow gold color, or yellowish brown, sometimes of an apple green or emerald color. It occurs crystallized in rectangular prisms, in imperfect octahedronsIts structure is lamellar, and it yields to the knife.

An ore or phosphate of uranium, called also uran- glimmer, and uran-mica. It is of a lemon yellow gold color, or yellowish brown, sometimes of an apple green or emerald color. It occurs crystallized in rectangular prisms, in imperfect octahedronsIts structure is lamellar, and it yields to the knife. Uranite is found in primitive earths, in three states, crystallized, compact, and pulverulent.
uranitic: Pertaining to uranite, or resembling it.
uranium: A metal discovered in 1789 by Klaproth, in the mineral called pechblend. It is occasionally found native in uran-ocher and uran- mica; but more generally it is obtained from pechblend, in which it exists with iron, copper, lead, and sometimes with arsenic, cobalt and zink.
uran-ocher: Pechblend, an ore of uranium, containing the metal in an oxydized state. It is brown, grayish, black, and brownish black; occurring massive globular, reniform, disseminated, and pulverulent.
uranology: A discourse or treatise on the heavens.
urbanity: That civility or courtesy of manners which is acquired by associating with well bred people; politeness; polished manners.
urbanize: To render civil and courteous; to polish.
urceolate: In botany, shaped like a pitcher; swelling out like a pitcher; as a calyx or corol.
urchin: A name of slight anger given to a child; as, the little urchin cried.
ureter: A tube conveying the urine from the kidney to the bladder. There are two ureters, one on each side.
urethra: The canal by which the urine is conducted from the bladder and discharged.
urge: To press; to push; to drive; to impel; to apply force to, in almost any manner. And great Achilles urge the Trojan fate.
To press the mind or will; to press by motives, arguments, persuasion or importunity. My broth did urge me in his act.
To provoke; to exasperate. Urge not my father’s anger.
To follow close; to impel. Heir urges heir, like wave impelling wave.
To labor vehemently; to press with eagerness. Through the thick deserts headlong urg’d his flight.
urgency: Pressure; importunity; earnest solicitation; as the urgency of a request.
Pressure of necessity; as the urgency of want or distress; the urgency of the occasion.
urgent: Pressing with necessity; violent; vehement; as an urgent case or occasion.
urgently: With pressing importunity; violently; vehemently; forcibly.
urger: One who urges; one who importunes.
urging: a. Pressing with solicitations; importunate. URGIING.
uric: In chimistry, the uric acid, called also lithic acid, is obtained from urinary calculi.
urim: The Urim and Thummim, among the Israelites, signify lights and perfections. These were a kind of ornament belonging to the habit of the high priest, in virtue of which he gave oracular answers to the people; but what they were has not been satisfactorily ascertained.
urinal: A bottle in which urine is kept for inspection.
In chimistry, an oblong glass vessel, used in making solutions.
urinary: Pertaining to urine; as the urinary bladder; urinary calculi; urinary abscesses.
In agriculture, a reservoir or place for the reception of urinefor manure.
urinarium: In agriculture, a reservoir or place for the reception of urinefor manure.
urinator: A diver; one who plunges and sinks in water in search of something, as for pearls.
urine: An animal fluid or liquor secreted by the kidneys, whence it is conveyed into the bladder by the ureters, and through the urethra discharged. The urine of beasts is sometimes called stale.
urinous: Pertaining to urine, or partaking of its qualities.
urn: A kind of vase of a roundish form, largest in the middle; used as an ornament.
A vessel in which the ashes of the dead were formerly kept.
A Roman measure for liquids, containing about three gallons and a half, wine measure. It was half the amphora, and four times the congius.
urry: A sort of blue or black clay, lying near a vein of coal.
ursa: The bear, a constellation, the greater and lesser bear, near the north pole.
ursiform: In the shape of a bear.
ursine: Pertaining to or resembling a bear.
ursuline: Denoting an order of nuns who observe the rule of St. Austin; so called from their institutress, St. Ursula.
us: objective case of we. Give us this day our daily bread.
usage: Treatment; an action or series of actions performed by one person towards another, or which directly affect him; as good usage; ill usage; hard usage. Gentle usage will often effect what harsh usage will not. The elephant may be governed by mild usage.

Use, or long continued use; custom; practice. Uninterrupted usage for a long time, or immemorial usage constitutes prescription. Custom is a local usage; prescription is a personal usage. In language, usage is the foundation of all rules. Of things once received and confirmed by use, long usage is a law sufficient.
usager: s as z. One who has the use of any thing in trust for another.
usance: In commerce, a determinate time fixed for the payment of bills of exchange, reckoned either from the day of their date, or the day of their acceptance. It is thus called because this time is settled by usage, or the custom of places on which the bills are drawn. In France, the usance for bills drawn from Spain and Portugal, is sixty days. At London, the usance for bills drawn from Holland, Germany or France is one month. The usance is very different in different countries and cities.
use: The act of handling or employing in any manner, and for any purpose, but especially for a profitable purpose; as the use of a pen in writing; the use of books in study; the use of a spade in digging. Use is of two kinds; that which employs a thing, without destroying it or its form, as the use of a book or of a farm; or it is the employment of a thing which destroys or wastes it, as the use of bread for provision; the use of water for turning a mill.
Employment; application of any thing to a purpose, good or bad. It is our duty to make a faithful use of our opportunities and advantages for improvement. Books can never teach the use of books.
Usefulness; utility; advantage; production of benefit. the value of a thing is to be estimated by its use. His friendship has been of use to me. Tis use alone that sanctifies expense.
Need of employment, or occasion to employ. I have no further use for this book.
used: s as z. Employed; occupied; treated.
useful: Producing or having power to produce good; beneficial; profitable; helpful towards advancing any purpose; as vessels and instruments useful in a family; books useful for improvement; useful knowledge; useful arts.
usefully: In such a manner as to produce or advance some end; as instruments or time usefully employed.
usefulness: Conduciveness to some end, properly to some valuable end; as the usefulness of canal navigation; the usefulness of machinery in manufactures.
useless: Having no use; unserviceable; producing no good end; answering no valuable purpose; not advancing the end proposed; as a useless garment; useless pity.
uselessly: In a useless manner; without profit or advantage.
uselessness: Unserviceableness; unfitness for any valuable purpose, or for the purpose intended; as the uselessness of pleasure.
user: s as z. One who uses, treats or occupies.
usher: Properly, an officer or servant who has the care of the door of a court, hall, chamber or the like; hence, an officer whose business is to introduce strangers, or to walk before a person of rank. In the king’s household there are four gentlemen-ushers of the privy chamber. There is also an usher of the exchequer, who attends the barons, sheriffs, juries.
An under-teacher or assistant to the preceptor of a school.
To introduce, as a forerunner or harbinger; to forerun. The stars that usher evening. The Examiner was ushered into the world by a letter, setting forth the great genius of the author.
usquebaugh: A compound distilled spirit. From this word, by corruption, we have whiskey.
ustion: The act of burning; the state of being burnt.
ustulation: The act of burning or searing.
In metallurgy, ustulation is the operation of expelling one substance from another by heat, as sulphur and arsenic from ores, in a muffle.
In pharmacy, the roasting or drying of moist substances so as to prepare them for pulverizing; also, the burning of wine.
usual: s as z. Customary; common; frequent; such as occurs in ordinary practice, or in the ordinary course of events. Rainy weather is not usual in this climate. Consultation with oracles was formerly a thing very usual.
usually: s as z. Commonly; customarily; ordinarily. Men usually find some excuse for their vices. It is usually as cold in North America in the fortieth degree of latitude, as it is in the west of Europe in the fiftieth.
usucaption: In the civil law, the same as prescription in the common law; the acquisition of the title or right to property by the uninterrupted and undisputed possession of it for a certain term prescribed by law.
usufruct: The temporary use and enjoyment of lands or tenements; or the right of receiving the fruits and profits of lands or other thing, without having the right to alienate or change the property.
usufructuary: A person who has the use and enjoyment of property for a time, without having the title or property.
usure: s as z. To practice usury.
usurer: Formerly, a person who lent money and took interest for it.
In present usage, one who lends money at a rate of interest beyond the rate established by law.
usurious: Practicing usury; taking exorbitant interest for the use of money; as a usurious person.
Partaking of usury; containing usury; as a usurious contract, which by statute is void.
usuriousness: The state or quality of being usurious.
usurp: s as z. To seize and hold in possession by force or without right; as, to usurp a throne; to usurp the prerogatives of the crown; to usurp power. To usurp the right of a patron, is to oust or dispossess him. Vice sometimes usurps the place of virtue.
usurpation: The act of seizing or occupying and enjoying the property of another, without right; as the usurpation of a throne; the usurpation of the supreme power. Usurpation, in a peculiar sense, denotes the absolute ouster and dispossession of the patron of a church, by presenting a clerk to a vacant benefice, who is thereupon admitted and instituted.
usurped: Seized or occupied and enjoyed by violence, or without right.
usurper: One who seizes or occupies the property of another without right; as the usurper of a throne, of power, or of the rights of a patron.
usurping: Seizing or occupying the power or property of another without right. The worst of tyrants, an usurping crowd.
usurpingly: By usurpation; without just right or claim.
usury: Formerly, interest; or a premium paid or stipulated to be paid for the use of money.
In present usage, illegal interest; a premium or compensation paid or stipulated to be paid for the use of money borrowed or retained, beyond the rate of interest established by law.
The practice of taking interest. Obs.
utensil: An instrument; that which is used; particularly, an instrument or vessel used in a kitchen, or in domestic and farming business.
uterine: Pertaining to the womb. Uterine brother or sister, is one born of the same mother, but by a different father.
utero-gestation: Gestation in the womb from conception to birth.
utility: Usefulness; production of good; profitableness to some valuable end; as the utility of manures upon land; the utility of the sciences; the utility of medicines.
utmost: V is the twenty second letter of the English Alphabet, and a labial articulation, formed by the junction of the upper teeth with the lower lip, as in pronouncing av, ev, ovIt is not a close articulation, but one that admits of some sound. It is nearly allied to F, being formed by the same organs; but V is vocal, and F is aspirate, and this constitutes the principal difference between them. V and U were formerly the same letter, derived no doubt from the oriental vau or waw, but they have now as distinct uses as any two letters in the alphabet, and are therefore to be considered as different letters. V has one sound only, as in ver, vote
Extreme; being at the furthest point or extremity; as the utmost limit of North America; the utmost limits of the land; the utmost extent of human knowledge.
Being in the greatest or highest degree; as the utmost assiduity; the utmost harmony; the utmost misery or happiness; the utmost peril.
The most that can be; the greatest power, degree or effort. He has done his utmost. Try your utmost. I will be free even to the utmost as I please in words.
umbratille: Being in retirement; secluded; as an umbratile life. UMBRATILE.
unbeguille: To undeceive; to free from the influence of deceit. Then unbeguile thyself. UNBEGUILE.
unconfinable: That cannot be confined or restrained.
unconfined: Not confined; free from restraint; free from control.
undecayving: Not decaying; not suffering diminution or decline.
unfortunately: Without success; unsuccessfully; unhappily. the scheme unfortunately miscarried.
unfortunateness: Ill luck; ill fortune; fortune; failure of success.
unfostered: Not countenanced by favor; not patronized.
unfouled: Not fouled; not polluted; not soiled; not corrupted; pure.
unfounded: Not founded; not built or established.
Having no foundation; vain; idle; as unfounded expectations.
unframable: Not to be framed or molded. commander; unfortunate business.
unframableness: The quality of not being framable.
unframed: Not framed; not fitted for erection; as unframed timber.
Not formed; not constructed; not fashioned.
unfrequent: Not frequent; not common; not happening often; infrequent.
unfrequented: Rarely visited; seldom resorted to by human beings; as an unfrequented place or forest.
unfriended: unfrend’ed. Wanting friends; not countenanced or supported.
unfriendly: Not friendly; not kind or benevolent; as an unfriendly neighbor.
Not favorable; not adapted to promote or support any object; as weather unfriendly to health.
unfrugal: Not frugal; not saving or economical.
unfruitful: Not producing fruit; barren; as an unfruitful tree.
Not producing off-spring; not prolific; barren; as an unfruitful female.
Not producing good effects or works; as an unfruitful life.
Unproductive; not fertile; as an unfruitful soil.
unfruitfulness: Barrenness; infecundity; unproductiveness; applied to persons or things.
unfulfilled: Not fulfilled; not accomplished; as a prophecy or prediction unfulfilled.
unfunded: Not funded; having no permanent funds for the payment of its interest; as an unfunded debt.
unfurl: To loose and unfold; to expand; to open or spread; as, to unfurl sails.
unfurnish: To strip of furniture; to divest; to strip.
unfurnished: Not furnished; not supplied with furniture; as an unfurnished room or house.
unfused: s as z. Not fused; not melted.
ungainly: Not expert or dextrous; clumsy; awkward; uncouth; as an ungainly strut in walking.
ungarrisoned: Not garrisoned; not furnished with troops for defense.
ungathered: Not gathered; not cropped; not picked.
ungear: To unharness; to strip of gear.
unhouse: To drive from the house or habitation; to dislodge.
unhoused: Driven from a house or habitation.
Destitute of shelter or cover. Cattle in severe weather should not be left unhoused.
unhouseled: s as z. Not having received the sacrament.
unhumbled: Not humbled; not affected with shame or confusion; not contrite in spirit .
In theology, not having the will and the natural enmity of the heart to God and his law.
unhurt: Not hurt; not harmed; free from wound or injury.
unicapsular: Having one capsule to each flower, as a pericarp.
unicorn: an animal with one horn; the monoceros. this name is often applied to the rhinoceros.
The sea unicorn is a fish of the whale kind, called narwal, remarkable for a horn growing out at his nose.
A fowl. Fossil unicorn, or fossil unicorn’s horn, a substance used in medicine, a terrene crustaceous spar.
uniflorous: Bearing one flower only; as a uniflorous peduncle.
uniform: Having always the same form or manner; not variable. Thus we say, the dress of the Asiatics is uniform, or has been uniform from early ages. so we Say, it is the uty of a christian to observe a uniform course of piety and religion.
Consistent with itself; not different; as, one’s opinions on a particular subject have been uniform.
Of the same form with others; consonant; agreeing with each other; conforming to one rule or mode. How far churches are bound to be uniform in their ceremonies, is doubted.
uniformity: resemblance to itself at all times; even tenor; as the uniformity of design in a poem.
consistency; sameness; as the uniformity of a man’s opinions.
Conformity to a pattern or rule; resemblance, consonance or agreement; as the uniformity of different churches in ceremonies or rites.
Similitude between the parts of a whole; as the uniformity of sides in a regular figure. Beauty is said to consist in uniformity with variety; .

Continued or unvaried sameness’ or likeness. Uniformity must tire at last, though it is a uniformity of excellence. Act of uniformity, in England, the act of parliament by which the form of public prayers, administration of sacraments and other rites, is prescribed to be observed in all the churches.
uniformly: With even tenor; without variation; as a temper uniformly mild.
Without diversity of one from another.
unigeniture: The state of being the only begotten.
unigenous: Of one kind; of the same genus.
unilabiate: In botany, having one lip only, as a corol.
unilateral: Being on one side or party’ only.
Having one side. A unilateral raceme, is when the flowers grow only on one side of the common peduncle.
unilocular: Having one cell only; as a unilocular pericarp.
unimaginable: Not to be imagined; not to be conceived.
unimaginably: To a degree not to be imagined.
unimpairable: Not liable to waste or diminution.
uminpaired: Not impaired; not diminished; not enfeebled by time or injury; as an unimpaired constitution.
unimpassioned: Free from passion; calm; not violent; as an unimpassioned address.
unimpeachable: That cannot be called in question; as an unimpeachable claim or testimony.
unimpeached: Not impeached not charged or accused; fair; as an unimpeached character.
Not called in question; as testimony unimpeached.' .
unimplied: Not implied; not included by fair inference.
unimportant: Not important; not of great moment.
unimpregnated: That cannot be impeached; that cannot be accused; free from stain, guilt or fault; as an unimpeachable reputation.
unimpressive: Not impressive; not forcible; not adapted to affect or awaken the passions.
Free from any temporary estate or interest, or from mortgage, or other charge or debt; as an estate unincumbered with dower.
unpropitious: Not propitious; not favorable; not disposed to promote; inauspicious.
unproposed: s as z. Not proposed; not offered.
unpropped: Not propped; not supported or upheld.
unprosperous: Not prosperous; not attended with success; unfortunate.
unprosperousness: Want of success; failure of the desired result.
unprotracted: Not protracted; not drawn out in length.
unproved: Not proved; not known by trial.
Not established as true by argument, demonstration or evidence.
unprovide: To unfurnish; to divest or strip of qualifications.
unprovided: Divested of qualifications. 2 .a Not provided; unfurnished; unsupplied.
unprovisioned: s as z. Not furnished with provisions.
unprovoked: Not provoked; not incited; applied to persons.
Not proceeding from provocation or just cause; as an unprovoked attack.
unpublic: Not public; private; not generally seen or known.
unpublished: Not published; as a manuscript or book.:
unpunctual: Not punctual; not exact in time.
unpunished: Not punished; suffered to pass without punishment or with impunity; as a thief unpunished; an unpunished crime.
unpurified: Not purified; not freed from recrement or foul matter.
unpursued: Not pursued; not followed; not prosecuted.
unqualified: Not qualified; not fit; not having the requisite talents, abilities or accomplishments.
Not having taken the requisite oath or oaths.
Not modified or restricted by conditions or exceptions; as unqualified praise.
unqueen: To divest of the dignity of queen.
unquenchable: That cannot be quenched; that will never be extinguished; inextinguishable. Matthew 3:12; Luke 3:17.
unquenchableness: The state or quality of being inextinguishable.
unquenchably: In a manner or degree so as not to be quenched.
unquestionable: a2. Not to be questioned; not to be doubted; indubitable; certain; as unquestionable evidence or truth; unquestionable courage.
unsightliness: Disagreeableness to the sight; deformity; ugliness.
unsightly: Disagreeable to the eye; ugly; deformed.
unsilvered: Not covered with quicksilver; as an unsilvered mirror.
unsincere: Not sound; not solid. [Obsolete in the two last significations, and for the first, insincere is generally used. See Insincerity.I .
unsinewed: or a. Deprived of strength or force; weak; nerveless.
unsinning: Committing no sin; impeccable; untainted with sin; as unsinning obedience.
unsizable: Not being of the proper size, magnitude or bulk.
unskilled: Wanting — skill; destitute of readiness or dexterity in performance.
unskillful: Not skillful; wanting the knowledge and dexterity which are acquired by observation, use and experience; as an unskillful surgeon; an unskillful mechanic; an unskillful logician.
unskillfully: Without skill, knowledge or dexterity; clumsily.
unskillfulness: Want of art or knowledge; want of that readiness in action or execution, which is acquired by use, experience and observation.
unslaked: Not slaked; unquenched; as unslaked thirst.
Not saturated with water; as unslaked lime.
unsleeping: Not adhering constantly to any fixed plan or business.
unsling: é. In seamen’s language, to take off the slings of a yard, a cask
unslipping: Not slipping; not liable to slip.
unslumbering: Never sleeping or slumbering; always watching or vigilant.
unsmirched: Not stained; not soiled or blacked.
unsmoked: Not smoked; not dried in smoke.
Not used in smoking, as a pipe.
unsociable: Not suitable to society; not having the qualities which are proper for society, and which render it agreeable; as an unsociable temper.
Not apt to converse; not free in conversation; reserved.
unsocial: Not adapted to society; not beneficial to society.
unsocket: To loose or take from a socket.
unsoiled: Not disgraced; not tainted; as character.
unsold: Not sold; not transferred for a consideration.
unsoldiered: Not having the qualities of a soldier.
unsolicited: Not asked for; as an unsolicited favor.
unsolicitous: Not solicitous; not anxious; not very desirous.
unsolid: Not solid; not firm; not substantial; as unsolid arguments or reasoning; an unsolid foundation.
unwhipt: Not whipped; not corrected with the rod.
utopian: Ideal; chimerical; fanciful; not well founded.
utricle: A little bag or bladder; a little cell; a reservoir in plants to receive the sap.
A capsule of one cell, and containing a solitary seed, often very thin and semitransparent, constantly destitute of valves, and falling with the seed.
utricular: Containing utricles; furnished with glandular vessels like small bags; as plants.
utter: Situated on the outside or remote from the center.
Placed or being beyond any compass; out of any place; as the utter deep.
Extreme; excessive; utmost; as utter darkness.
Complete; total; final; as utter ruin.
Peremptory; absolute; as an utter refusal or denial.
utterable: That may be uttered, pronounced or expressed.
utterance: The act of uttering words; pronunciation; manner of speaking; as a good or bad utterance. They began to speak with other tongues, as the spirit gave them utterance. Acts 2:'4.
Emission from the mouth; vocal expression; as the utterance of sounds.
uttered: Spoken; pronounced; disclosed; published; put into circulation.
utterer: One who utters; one who pronounces.
uttering: Pronouncing; disclosing; putting into circulation; selling.
utterly: To the full extent; fully; perfectly; totally; as utterly tired; utterly debased; utterly lost to all sense of shame; It is utterly vain; utterly out of my power.
uttermost: The greatest. the uttermost we can do is to be patient. To the uttermost, in the most extensive degree; fully. Hebrews 7:25.
Extreme; being in the furthest, greatest or highest degree; as the uttermost extent or end; the uttermost distress.
uvula: A soft round spungy body, suspended from the palate near the foramina of the nostrils, over the glottis. The small conical body projecting from the middle of the soft palate.
uxoriously: With fond or servile submission to a wife.
uxoriousness: Connubial dotage; foolish fondness for a wife. V V is the twenty second letter of the English Alphabet, and a labial articulation, formed by the junction of the upper teeth with the lower lip, as in pronouncing av, ev, ovIt is nota close articulation, but one that admits of some sound. It is nearly allied to F, being formed by the same organs; but V is vocal, and F is aspirate, and this constitutes the principal difference between them. V and U were formerly the same letter, derived no doubt from the oriental vau or waw, but they have now as distinct uses as any two letters in the alphabet, and are therefore to be considered as different letters. V has one sound only, as in ver, voteAs a numeral, V stand for 5. With a dash over it, in old books, it stands for 5000. V.
